notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This is one of the most important passages in the Laws, establishing its central political theology. The shepherd analogy (superior beings ruling inferior ones) appears in the Statesman (271d-275a) but is used there more critically; the Statesman ultimately rejects the shepherd model for human politics, while the Laws uses it to argue for the rule of law as a substitute for divine governance. The etymology connecting nomos (law) to nous (mind/reason) is philosophically significant: law is not mere convention but the embodiment of rational order. The description of the insatiable soul echoes Republic IX (571b-572b) on the tyrannical soul driven by lawless desires. Compare Timaeus (90a-d) on the divine element in human nature that must be cultivated.

narrative: "You are aware -- are you not? -- that there are said to be as many forms of laws as there are of governments, and of the latter we have already mentioned all those which are commonly recognized. Now you must regard this as a matter of first-rate importance. For what is to be the standard of just and unjust, is once more the point at issue. Men say that the law ought not to regard either military virtue, or virtue in general, but only the interests and power and preservation of the established form of government; this is thought by them to be the best way of expressing the natural definition of justice."
refashioned:
"You are aware, I trust, that there are said to be as many kinds of law as there are forms of government -- and we have already listed all the commonly recognized forms. Now, you must treat this as a matter of the highest importance. The real question is: what will serve as the standard of just and unjust?

Some people say that law should concern itself not with military virtue, or with virtue of any kind, but solely with the interests, power, and preservation of whichever form of government happens to be in place. They consider this the best expression of the natural definition of justice."
elegant: There are as many kinds of law as forms of government, and the critical question is the standard of justice. Some hold that law should serve only the interests and preservation of whatever regime is in power -- not virtue of any kind -- and call this the natural definition of justice.
summary: The Athenian raises the fundamental question of what standard should define justice in law. He identifies a common view that law should serve only the preservation of the ruling power, regardless of virtue -- effectively defining justice as the interest of the established regime.
distilled: The central question of legislation is the standard of justice, and the common but false view is that justice means whatever preserves the ruling power.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage introduces the Thrasymachean thesis -- that justice is the interest of the stronger -- which Plato had already engaged at length in Republic I (338c-339a). Thrasymachus argued that justice is nothing more than what benefits the ruling class, and the Athenian here presents the same position as a common political assumption. The phrase "natural definition of justice" is ironic: what is presented as natural is actually the corruption of true justice. The Laws' answer will be that justice is obedience to law that embodies reason (nous), not service to any particular faction or regime.

narrative:
"How?"

"Justice is said by them to be the interest of the stronger."

"Speak plainer."

"I will: -- 'Surely,' they say, 'the governing power makes whatever laws have authority in any state?'"

"True."

"'Well,' they would add, 'and do you suppose that tyranny or democracy, or any other conquering power, does not make the continuance of the power which is possessed by them the first or principal object of their laws?'"

"How can they have any other?"

"'And whoever transgresses these laws is punished as an evil-doer by the legislator, who calls the laws just?'"

"Naturally."

"'This, then, is always the mode and fashion in which justice exists.'"

"Certainly, if they are correct in their view."

"Why, yes, this is one of those false principles of government to which we were referring."

"Which do you mean?"

"Those which we were examining when we spoke of who ought to govern whom. Did we not arrive at the conclusion that parents ought to govern their children, and the elder the younger, and the noble the ignoble? And there were many other principles, if you remember, and they were not always consistent. One principle was this very principle of might, and we said that Pindar considered violence natural and justified it."

"Yes; I remember."

"Consider, then, to whom our state is to be entrusted. For there is a thing which has occurred times without number in states --"

"What thing?"
refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Justice, they say, is the interest of the stronger."

"Explain more clearly."

"I will. 'Surely,' they argue, 'the governing power in any state makes whatever laws have authority there.'"

"True."

"'And do you suppose,' they continue, 'that any conquering power -- whether tyranny or democracy -- does not make the continuation of its own power the first and principal object of its laws?'"

"What else could they aim at?"

"'And whoever breaks these laws is punished as a criminal by the legislator, who calls the laws just?'"

"Naturally."

"'This, then, is always the way justice works.'"

"Certainly, if their view is correct."

"But this is precisely one of those false principles of government we discussed earlier."

"Which do you mean?"

"The ones we examined when we asked who ought to govern whom. We concluded that parents should govern children, elders should govern the young, and the noble should govern the ignoble. There were many such principles, and they were not always consistent. One of them was this very principle of might, and we noted that Pindar considered violence natural and justified it."

"Yes, I remember."

"Consider, then, to whom our state should be entrusted. For there is something that has happened countless times in states --"

"What is it?"
elegant: The argument that justice is the interest of the stronger runs as follows: the ruling power makes the laws; every regime makes its own preservation the chief aim of its laws; whoever breaks those laws is punished as a criminal; therefore justice simply means obedience to the ruling power. The Athenian identifies this as a false principle, recalling their earlier discussion of competing claims to rule -- including Pindar's view that violence is natural and justified -- and turns to the question of who should govern the new colony.
summary: The Athenian presents and then rejects the argument that justice is the interest of the stronger: every regime legislates to preserve its own power and punishes those who resist. He links this to earlier discussions of competing claims to authority, including Pindar's justification of natural violence, and pivots to who should govern the new state.
distilled: The thesis that justice is the interest of the stronger -- that every regime simply legislates to maintain its own power -- is identified as a false principle that must be replaced by a truer standard of political authority.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The argument presented here closely mirrors Thrasymachus's position in Republic I (338c-341a). Pindar's fragment about violence (nomos) being "king of all" and making "the most violent appear just" was discussed in Laws III (690b-c). The reference to competing principles of rule (parents over children, elder over younger, noble over ignoble) recalls Laws III (689e-690d), where seven distinct claims to political authority were enumerated. The Athenian's identification of this position as "false" sets up the alternative: that legitimate authority must be grounded in law and virtue rather than mere power.

narrative:
"That when there has been a contest for power, those who gain the upper hand so entirely monopolize the government, as to refuse all share to the defeated party and their descendants -- they live watching one another, the ruling class being in perpetual fear that some one who has a recollection of former wrongs will come into power and rise up against them. Now, according to our view, such governments are not polities at all, nor are laws right which are passed for the good of particular classes and not for the good of the whole state. States which have such laws are not polities but parties, and their notions of justice are simply unmeaning. I say this, because I am going to assert that we must not entrust the government in your state to any one because he is rich, or because he possesses any other advantage, such as strength, or stature, or again birth: but he who is most obedient to the laws of the state, he shall win the palm; and to him who is victorious in the first degree shall be given the highest office and chief ministry of the gods; and the second to him who bears the second palm; and on a similar principle shall all the others be assigned to those who come next in order. And when I call the rulers servants or ministers of the law, I give them this name not for the sake of novelty, but because I certainly believe that upon such service or ministry depends the well- or ill-being of the state. For that state in which the law is subject and has no authority, I perceive to be on the highway to ruin; but I see that the state in which the law is above the rulers, and the rulers are the inferiors of the law, has salvation, and every blessing which the Gods can confer."

"Truly, Stranger, you see with the keen vision of age."

"Why, yes; every man when he is young has that sort of vision dullest, and when he is old keenest."

"Very true."

"And now, what is to be the next step? May we not suppose the colonists to have arrived, and proceed to make our speech to them?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"When there has been a struggle for power, the victors monopolize the government so completely that they refuse any share to the defeated party or their descendants. Both sides live in constant suspicion -- the ruling class perpetually afraid that someone with a memory of past wrongs will rise up against them.

In our view, such governments are not true constitutions at all, and laws passed for the benefit of one class rather than the whole state are not legitimate laws. States with such laws are not polities but factions, and their conceptions of justice are meaningless.

I say this because I intend to assert that in your state, no one should be entrusted with government because he is rich, or strong, or tall, or well-born. Instead, the person most obedient to the state's laws shall win the highest place. The most law-abiding citizen shall receive the highest office and the chief service of the gods. The second most obedient shall receive the second-highest office, and so on in order.

When I call rulers 'servants' or 'ministers' of the law, I do not use this term for novelty's sake. I genuinely believe that the well-being or ruin of the state depends on this distinction. Where the law is subordinate and has no real authority, I see a state on the road to destruction. But where the law stands above the rulers, and the rulers are subordinate to the law -- there I see salvation and every blessing the gods can bestow."

"Truly, Stranger, you see with the keen vision of age."

"Indeed -- when a man is young, his vision for such things is at its dullest; when he is old, at its sharpest."

"Very true."

"Now, what is the next step? Shall we suppose that the colonists have arrived, and proceed to address them?"

"Certainly."
elegant: When victors in political struggle monopolize power and exclude the defeated, the result is faction, not a true constitution, and their concept of justice is meaningless. Government should be entrusted not to the rich, strong, or wellborn, but to whoever is most obedient to the laws. Rulers must be servants and ministers of law -- not a mere phrase but a foundational principle. Where law is subordinate to rulers, the state is on the road to ruin; where law stands above the rulers, there lies salvation and every divine blessing.
summary: The Athenian argues that factional governments that exclude the defeated are not true constitutions, and their justice is meaningless. In the new colony, authority must go to whoever is most obedient to law, with rulers serving as ministers of law. The key principle: where law rules over rulers, the state is saved; where rulers rule over law, the state is doomed.
distilled: Where the law is sovereign over rulers, there is salvation; where rulers are sovereign over law, there is ruin -- so authority must go to whoever is most obedient to the laws, not to the rich or wellborn.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (with Cleinias responding). -- The declaration that rulers are "servants of the law" (hyperetai tou nomou) is one of the most influential formulations in Western political philosophy, anticipating the modern concept of the rule of law. It stands in deliberate contrast to the Thrasymachean position just refuted. The principle that law must be sovereign echoes Aristotle's Politics (III.16, 1287a): "He who bids the law rule may be deemed to bid God and Reason alone rule, but he who bids man rule adds an element of the beast." The vision metaphor -- young eyes dull, old eyes keen for political truth -- inverts the usual Greek association of youth with sharp sight. The transition to addressing the colonists marks the beginning of the great prelude (prooimion) to the Laws' legislation.

narrative:
"'Friends,' we say to them, -- 'God, as the old tradition declares, holding in his hand the beginning, middle, and end of all that is, travels according to his nature in a straight line towards the accomplishment of his end. Justice always accompanies him, and is the punisher of those who fall short of the divine law. To justice, he who would be happy holds fast, and follows in her company with all humility and order; but he who is lifted up with pride, or elated by wealth or rank, or beauty, who is young and foolish, and has a soul hot with insolence, and thinks that he has no need of any guide or ruler, but is able himself to be the guide of others, he, I say, is left deserted of God; and being thus deserted, he takes to him others who are like himself, and dances about, throwing all things into confusion, and many think that he is a great man, but in a short time he pays a penalty which justice cannot but approve, and is utterly destroyed, and his family and city with him. Wherefore, seeing that human things are thus ordered, what should a wise man do or think, or not do or think?'"

"Every man ought to make up his mind that he will be one of the followers of God; there can be no doubt of that."
refashioned:
"'Friends,' we will say to the colonists, 'God, as the ancient tradition tells us, holds in his hand the beginning, middle, and end of all that exists, and travels in a straight line toward the fulfillment of his purpose, as his nature requires. Justice always walks at his side, punishing those who fall short of the divine law.

The person who would be happy holds fast to Justice and follows in her company with humility and order. But the one who is swollen with pride -- elated by wealth, rank, or beauty, young and foolish, with a soul burning with insolence, who believes he needs no guide or ruler and can himself be the guide of others -- that person is abandoned by God. Once abandoned, he gathers others like himself, and they dance about wildly, throwing everything into confusion. Many think he is a great man. But in a short time he pays a penalty that Justice approves, and he is utterly destroyed -- together with his family and his city.

Seeing that human affairs are ordered this way, what should a wise person do or think?'"

"Every person should resolve to be a follower of God. There can be no doubt about that."
elegant: God moves in a straight line toward his purpose, with Justice always at his side punishing those who violate divine law. The humble and orderly person who follows Justice will be happy. But whoever is swollen with pride -- by wealth, rank, or beauty -- and thinks he needs no guide, is abandoned by God, gathers others like himself, throws everything into chaos, and is soon utterly destroyed along with his family and city. The wise resolve to follow God.
summary: The Athenian begins the great address to the colonists with a theological proclamation: God moves purposefully with Justice at his side. The humble who follow Justice prosper; the proud who think they need no guidance are abandoned by God, sow chaos, and are destroyed along with everything they touch.
distilled: God moves purposefully with Justice at his side; the humble who follow prosper, while the proud who think they need no guide are abandoned and destroyed.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This is the opening of the great prelude (prooimion) to the Laws' legislation, one of the most elevated passages in all of Plato. The image of God holding "the beginning, middle, and end" may echo Orphic theology. The straight-line movement of God contrasts with the circling dance of the abandoned soul -- order versus chaos. The portrait of the proud man abandoned by God parallels the Republic's account of the tyrannical soul (IX, 571a-576b) and anticipates the Christian concept of spiritual pride. The phrase "dances about" (kordax) suggests the vulgar dance of comedy, implying that the self-important person is actually ridiculous. Compare Protagoras 345d on the difficulty of being truly good, and Laws Book 5 (716a-b) which continues this theological theme.

narrative: "Then what life is agreeable to God, and becoming in his followers? One only, expressed once for all in the old saying that 'like agrees with like, with measure measure,' but things which have no measure agree neither with themselves nor with the things which have. Now God ought to be to us the measure of all things, and not man, as men commonly say (Protagoras): the words are far more true of him. And he who would be dear to God must, as far as is possible, be like him and such as he is. Wherefore the temperate man is the friend of God, for he is like him; and the intemperate man is unlike him, and different from him, and unjust. And the same applies to other things; and this is the conclusion, which is also the noblest and truest of all sayings -- that for the good man to offer sacrifice to the Gods, and hold converse with them by means of prayers and offerings and every kind of service, is the noblest and best of all things, and also the most conducive to a happy life, and very fit and meet. But with the bad man, the opposite of this is true: for the bad man has an impure soul, whereas the good is pure; and from one who is polluted, neither good man nor God can without impropriety receive gifts. Wherefore the unholy do only waste their much service upon the Gods, but when offered by any holy man, such service is most acceptable to them. This is the mark at which we ought to aim. But what weapons shall we use, and how shall we direct them?"
refashioned:
"What life, then, is pleasing to God and fitting for his followers? Only one, captured in the ancient saying: 'like agrees with like, measure with measure' -- but what lacks measure agrees neither with itself nor with what has measure.

Now, God -- not man, as people commonly say following Protagoras -- ought to be for us the measure of all things. And that saying is far truer of God than of man. Whoever wishes to be dear to God must become, as far as possible, like God. Therefore the temperate person is God's friend, being like him, while the intemperate person is unlike God, different from him, and unjust. The same principle applies to everything else.

From this follows the noblest and truest of all conclusions: for the good person, offering sacrifice to the gods and holding communion with them through prayers, offerings, and every form of service is the noblest and best of all activities, the most conducive to a happy life, and entirely fitting. But for the bad person, the opposite is true. The bad person has an impure soul, while the good person's soul is pure. Neither God nor any good person can properly accept gifts from someone who is polluted. The unholy therefore waste all their elaborate worship on the gods, but when a holy person offers service, it is most welcome.

This is the target at which we must aim. But what weapons shall we use, and how shall we direct them?"
elegant: God, not man, is the measure of all things -- Protagoras was wrong to say otherwise. Like agrees with like: the temperate person is God's friend because they resemble God; the intemperate person is unlike God and therefore unjust. From this follows the noblest truth: worship from the good is the highest activity and most welcome to the gods, while worship from the corrupt is wasted, since neither God nor any good person can properly accept gifts from a polluted soul.
summary: The Athenian directly contradicts Protagoras's famous dictum: God, not man, is the measure of all things. Since like attracts like, the temperate person who resembles God becomes God's friend. The practical consequence is that worship and sacrifice from a good person are the noblest activities, while the same from a corrupt person are worthless.
distilled: God, not man, is the measure of all things; the temperate person resembles God and is therefore his friend, while the corrupt waste their worship because neither God nor good people can properly receive gifts from polluted souls.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The direct refutation of Protagoras's "man is the measure of all things" (homo mensura) is one of the most significant philosophical moments in the Laws. Protagoras's relativism, which Plato engaged at length in the Theaetetus (152a-186e), is here replaced with theological absolutism: God as the fixed standard of value. The principle that "like is dear to like" appears also in Lysis (214a-b) and Symposium (195b). The distinction between the worship of the good and the worship of the corrupt echoes Euthyphro (14c-15a) on the nature of piety and anticipates Laws Book 10's extended argument against atheism. The metaphor of aiming at a mark continues the archer imagery from 4.5.

narrative: "In the first place, we affirm that next after the Olympian Gods and the Gods of the State, honour should be given to the Gods below; they should receive everything in even and of the second choice, and ill omen, while the odd numbers, and the first choice, and the things of lucky omen, are given to the Gods above, by him who would rightly hit the mark of piety. Next to these Gods, a wise man will do service to the demons or spirits, and then to the heroes, and after them will follow the private and ancestral Gods, who are worshipped as the law prescribes in the places which are sacred to them. Next comes the honour of living parents, to whom, as is meet, we have to pay the first and greatest and oldest of all debts, considering that all which a man has belongs to those who gave him birth and brought him up, and that he must do all that he can to minister to them, first, in his property, secondly, in his person, and thirdly, in his soul, in return for the endless care and travail which they bestowed upon him of old, in the days of his infancy, and which he is now to pay back to them when they are old and in the extremity of their need. And all his life long he ought never to utter, or to have uttered, an unbecoming word to them; for of light and fleeting words the penalty is most severe; Nemesis, the messenger of justice, is appointed to watch over all such matters."
refashioned:
"First, we declare that after the Olympian gods and the gods of the state, honor should be given to the gods of the underworld. They should receive everything in even numbers and of second quality, and all things of ill omen, while odd numbers, the choicest offerings, and things of good omen are reserved for the gods above -- this is how one rightly aims at piety.

After these gods, a wise person will honor the daemons or spirits, then the heroes, and then the private and ancestral gods, worshipped as the law prescribes in their sacred places.

Next comes the honor owed to living parents -- the first, greatest, and oldest of all debts. Everything a person has belongs to those who gave them birth and raised them. One must serve them in every way possible: first with one's property, then with one's person, and finally with one's soul -- repaying the endless care and labor they devoted during one's infancy, now that they are old and in desperate need.

Throughout one's entire life, one should never speak, and never have spoken, a disrespectful word to them. For careless and thoughtless words carry the severest penalty: Nemesis, the messenger of justice, is appointed to oversee exactly such matters."
elegant: After the Olympian gods and state gods come the gods below, then daemons, heroes, and ancestral gods -- each honored in the proper manner and order. After the gods comes the honor of living parents: the first, greatest, and oldest of all debts. Everything one has belongs to those who gave birth and upbringing. One must repay their care with property, person, and soul, and must never speak a disrespectful word to them, for Nemesis watches over such things with the severest penalties.
summary: The Athenian establishes a hierarchy of religious and social obligations: Olympian gods first, then underworld gods, daemons, heroes, and ancestral gods. After all divine honors comes the duty to parents -- the oldest and greatest of all debts -- which must be repaid with property, personal service, and devotion of the soul.
distilled: After a hierarchy of divine honors comes the supreme human obligation: honoring parents as the oldest, greatest debt, repayable with property, person, and soul, under the watchful eye of Nemesis.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The hierarchy of honor (Olympians, chthonic gods, daemons, heroes, ancestral gods, parents) reflects standard Greek religious practice systematized for legislative purposes. The distinction between even/odd offerings and first/second quality for underworld vs. heavenly gods is based on actual Greek ritual practice. Nemesis as the guardian of filial piety draws on her traditional role as the goddess who punishes hubris and excess. The three-part service to parents (property, person, soul) creates a comprehensive duty that parallels the Laws' tripartite concern with possessions, bodies, and souls. Compare Republic IV (427b-c) on traditional religious observances and Laws Book 11 (930e-932d) for the formal laws governing treatment of parents.

narrative: "When they are angry and want to satisfy their feelings in word or deed, he should give way to them; for a father who thinks that he has been wronged by his son may be reasonably expected to be very angry. At their death, the most moderate funeral is best, neither exceeding the customary expense, nor yet falling short of the honour which has been usually shown by the former generation to their parents. And let a man not forget to pay the yearly tribute of respect to the dead, honouring them chiefly by omitting nothing that conduces to a perpetual remembrance of them, and giving a reasonable portion of his fortune to the dead. Doing this, and living after this manner, we shall receive our reward from the Gods and those who are above us [i.e., the demons]; and we shall spend our days for the most part in good hope. And how a man ought to order what relates to his descendants and his kindred and friends and fellow-citizens, and the rites of hospitality taught by Heaven, and the intercourse which arises out of all these duties, with a view to the embellishment and orderly regulation of his own life -- these things, I say, the laws, as we proceed with them, will accomplish, partly persuading, and partly when natures do not yield to the persuasion of custom, chastising them by might and right, and will thus render our state, if the Gods co-operate with us, prosperous and happy."
refashioned:
"When parents are angry and wish to vent their feelings in word or deed, a child should yield to them. A father who believes he has been wronged by his son can reasonably be expected to be furious.

At their death, the most moderate funeral is best -- neither exceeding the customary expense nor falling short of the honor previous generations have shown their parents. And one must not forget to pay the yearly tribute of respect to the dead, honoring them above all by omitting nothing that serves their perpetual remembrance, and devoting a reasonable portion of one's fortune to their memory.

By living this way, we shall receive our reward from the gods and the daemons who are above us, and we shall pass our days mostly in good hope.

As for how a person should manage relations with descendants, relatives, friends, fellow citizens, and the sacred duties of hospitality, and all the interactions arising from these obligations, with a view to adorning and regulating one's own life -- these matters the laws themselves will address as we develop them. The laws will accomplish this partly through persuasion and partly, when certain natures refuse to yield to the influence of custom, through justified coercion. In this way, if the gods cooperate with us, our state will be made prosperous and happy."
elegant: One should yield to angry parents and give them moderate funerals that match tradition. The yearly tribute of remembrance to the dead must never be neglected. By living this way, we earn the favor of gods and daemons. The remaining duties -- to descendants, kin, friends, citizens, and guests -- the laws will regulate through a combination of persuasion and, when necessary, justified force, rendering the state prosperous and happy with the gods' help.
summary: The Athenian completes the prelude's moral instruction: children must defer to angry parents, provide moderate funerals, and maintain annual remembrance of the dead. The remaining social obligations will be addressed by the laws themselves, which will use persuasion where possible and compulsion where necessary.
distilled: The duties to parents extend beyond their death through moderate funerals and perpetual remembrance, while the laws will regulate all remaining social obligations through a combination of persuasion and coercion.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The principle of yielding to angry parents reflects the Greek value of paternal authority, but the Athenian frames it psychologically (anger at perceived wrong) rather than as absolute deference. The moderation in funerals connects back to the earlier discussion of the mean in 4.36. The combination of persuasion and coercion as the twin instruments of law is the central methodological insight of Book 4, anticipating the formal distinction between legal preludes (persuasion) and legal commands (coercion) developed in 4.37-4.44. Compare Republic III (414b-c) on the noble lie as a form of civic persuasion, and Laws Book 10 (885d-907d) where both persuasion and punishment are deployed against atheism.

narrative:
"But of what has to be said, and must be said by the legislator who is of my way of thinking, and yet, if said in the form of law, would be out of place -- of this I think that he may give a sample for the instruction of himself and of those for whom he is legislating; and then when, as far as he is able, he has gone through all the preliminaries, he may proceed to the work of legislation. Now, what will be the form of such prefaces? There may be a difficulty in including or describing them all under a single form, but I think that we may get some notion of them if we can guarantee one thing."

"What is that?"

"I should wish the citizens to be as readily persuaded to virtue as possible; this will surely be the aim of the legislator in all his laws."

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"There are things the legislator of my persuasion must say, but which would be out of place if cast in the form of law. Of these, I think he can offer a sample to instruct both himself and the people he is legislating for. Once he has worked through these preliminaries as fully as he can, he may proceed to actual legislation.

Now, what form should such prefaces take? It may be difficult to capture them all under one description, but I think we can get a sense of them if we can establish one thing."

"What is that?"

"I would want the citizens to be as readily persuaded toward virtue as possible. Surely that will be the legislator's aim in all his laws."

"Certainly."
elegant: Some things the legislator must say cannot properly take the form of law -- they serve as prefaces that prepare citizens to receive legislation willingly. The aim of such prefaces is simple: to make citizens as readily persuadable toward virtue as possible.
summary: The Athenian introduces the concept of legislative prefaces: things the lawgiver must communicate that do not belong in the laws themselves but prepare citizens to accept legislation. The single aim of all such prefaces is to make citizens receptive to virtue.
distilled: The legislator needs prefaces to law -- persuasive preliminaries that prepare citizens' souls to receive legal commands with willingness rather than resistance.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This passage marks the transition from the content of the prelude to a theoretical reflection on the nature and function of preludes themselves. The concept of legislative preambles (prooimia) is one of the Laws' most original contributions to political philosophy. No previous Greek thinker had systematically theorized the distinction between the persuasive and coercive functions of law. The insight that citizens must be prepared to receive laws willingly connects to the broader Platonic theme that true compliance comes from understanding rather than fear -- paralleling the Republic's distinction between the education of guardians (Books II-III) and the enforcement of rules.

narrative: "The proposal appears to me to be of some value; and I think that a person will listen with more gentleness and good-will to the precepts addressed to him by the legislator, when his soul is not altogether unprepared to receive them. Even a little done in the way of conciliation gains his ear, and is always worth having. For there is no great inclination or readiness on the part of mankind to be made as good, or as quickly good, as possible. The case of the many proves the wisdom of Hesiod, who says that the road to wickedness is smooth and can be travelled without perspiring, because it is so very short:"
refashioned:
"This proposal seems valuable to me. A person will listen with greater gentleness and goodwill to the legislator's precepts when his soul is not entirely unprepared to receive them. Even a small effort at conciliation wins his ear and is always worthwhile.

For human beings have no great natural inclination or eagerness to become as good, or as quickly good, as possible. The behavior of the majority confirms the wisdom of Hesiod, who says that the road to wickedness is smooth and can be traveled without breaking a sweat, because it is so very short:"
elegant: A person listens with greater gentleness when his soul has been prepared to receive the legislator's precepts. Even slight conciliation wins the ear and is always worthwhile. Humans have no natural eagerness to become good quickly -- confirming Hesiod's observation that the road to wickedness is smooth and short.
summary: The Athenian argues that even modest efforts to prepare citizens' souls through persuasion make a significant difference, since people have no natural eagerness to become virtuous. He quotes Hesiod on the ease of wickedness to illustrate the uphill nature of moral improvement.
distilled: Even small efforts at persuasion prepare the soul to receive law with goodwill, which matters because humans have no natural eagerness for virtue -- the road to wickedness is smooth and short.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The Hesiod quotation is from Works and Days (287-289), one of the most frequently cited passages in Greek ethical thought. Plato uses it to ground his theory of legislative preambles in a realistic assessment of human nature: because virtue does not come naturally or easily, persuasion must do the preparatory work that coercion alone cannot accomplish. This realism distinguishes the Laws from the more idealistic Republic, which relies on a comprehensive education system to produce virtuous guardians. The concept of the soul being "not altogether unprepared" suggests degrees of readiness -- the preamble does not create virtue but removes obstacles to its reception.

narrative:
"But before virtue the immortal Gods have placed the sweat of labour, and long and steep is the way thither, and rugged at first; but when you have reached the top, although difficult before, it is then easy."

"Yes; and he certainly speaks well."

"Very true: and now let me tell you the effect which the preceding discourse has had upon me."

"Proceed."

"Suppose that we have a little conversation with the legislator, and say to him -- 'O, legislator, speak; if you know what we ought to say and do, you can surely tell.'"

"Of course he can."

"'Did we not hear you just now saying, that the legislator ought not to allow the poets to do what they liked? For that they would not know in which of their words they went against the laws, to the hurt of the state.'"

"That is true."

"May we not fairly make answer to him on behalf of the poets?"

"What answer shall we make to him?"
refashioned:
"'But before virtue the immortal gods have placed the sweat of labor, and long and steep is the way to it, and rough at first; yet once you reach the summit, what was difficult becomes easy.'"

"Yes, and Hesiod certainly speaks well."

"Indeed he does. Now let me tell you the effect our preceding discussion has had on me."

"Go on."

"Imagine that we approach the legislator and say: 'Legislator, speak to us. If you know what we ought to say and do, surely you can tell us.'"

"Of course he can."

"'Did we not just hear you say that the legislator ought not to let poets do as they please, because they would not know which of their words violated the laws and harmed the state?'"

"That is true."

"Then may we fairly offer a response on the poets' behalf?"

"What response shall we give?"
elegant: Hesiod's words confirm that virtue requires labor and a steep, rough climb, though it becomes easy at the summit. This prompts a question for the legislator: if poets must be restrained because they do not know when their words harm the state, may someone speak in the poets' defense?
summary: The Athenian completes the Hesiod quotation about virtue's difficult path, then pivots to a hypothetical conversation with the legislator about the restriction of poets. If poets are to be controlled because they unknowingly violate laws, the Athenian proposes to speak in their defense.
distilled: Having established that virtue's path is steep though rewarding, the Athenian sets up a defense of the poets against the legislator's impulse to restrict them.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The Hesiod quotation continues from Works and Days (289-292). The shift to discussing poets and the legislator connects back to Laws Book 2 (656c-660a) on the regulation of poetry and music, and more broadly to Republic II-III (376e-398b) and X (595a-608b) where poets are subjected to strict censorship. The Athenian's proposal to speak "on behalf of the poets" is surprising given Plato's reputation for hostility to poetry, but the argument will ultimately serve to distinguish the poet's freedom from the legislator's responsibility -- the poet may contradict himself, but the legislator cannot.

narrative:
"That the poet, according to the tradition which has ever prevailed among us, and is accepted of all men, when he sits down on the tripod of the muse, is not in his right mind; like a fountain, he allows to flow out freely whatever comes in, and his art being imitative, he is often compelled to represent men of opposite dispositions, and thus to contradict himself; neither can he tell whether there is more truth in one thing that he has said than in another. This is not the case in a law; the legislator must give not two rules about the same thing, but one only. Take an example from what you have just been saying. Of three kinds of funerals, there is one which is too extravagant, another is too niggardly, the third is a mean; and you choose and approve and order the last without qualification. But if I had an extremely rich wife, and she bade me bury her and describe her burial in a poem, I should praise the extravagant sort; and a poor miserly man, who had not much money to spend, would approve of the niggardly; and the man of moderate means, who was himself moderate, would praise a moderate funeral. Now you in the capacity of legislator must not barely say 'a moderate funeral,' but you must define what moderation is, and how much; unless you are definite, you must not suppose that you are speaking a language that can become law."

"Certainly not."
refashioned:
"The poet, according to the tradition that has always prevailed among us and is accepted by everyone, is not in his right mind when he sits upon the tripod of the Muse. Like a fountain, he lets whatever comes in flow freely out. Since his art is imitative, he is often compelled to portray characters of opposite dispositions, and so he contradicts himself. He cannot even say whether one of his statements is truer than another.

This is not how law works. The legislator must give not two rules about the same thing, but one and only one. Take your own example just now. Of three kinds of funeral -- one too extravagant, another too stingy, and a third that is moderate -- you chose and ordered the moderate without qualification. But if I had an extremely wealthy wife who asked me to bury her and describe her burial in a poem, I would praise the extravagant funeral. A poor, miserly man would approve of the cheapest. A man of moderate means and moderate character would praise the moderate funeral.

As a legislator, you cannot simply say 'a moderate funeral.' You must define what moderation is and specify how much. Unless you are precise, do not suppose you are speaking a language that can become law."

"Certainly not."
elegant: The poet, seated on the Muse's tripod, is not in his right mind -- like a fountain, he pours out whatever comes in and, because his art is imitative, must portray opposite characters and contradict himself. Law is fundamentally different: the legislator must give one rule, not two, about the same thing. It is not enough to prescribe 'moderation' -- the law must define precisely what moderation means and how much it costs, or it is not truly law.
summary: The Athenian contrasts poetry and legislation: the inspired poet contradicts himself because he must represent opposing characters, but the legislator must prescribe exactly one rule for each situation. Laws must be precise and definite -- saying 'be moderate' is not enough without specifying what moderation means in concrete terms.
distilled: The poet may contradict himself because inspiration and imitation require it, but the legislator must give one precise, unambiguous rule -- 'be moderate' is not law until moderation is defined.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The image of the poet on the Muse's tripod, pouring out inspiration like a fountain, closely parallels the Ion (533d-534e), where Socrates describes poets as divinely possessed channels who create without rational understanding. The contrast between poetic ambiguity and legal precision is a major contribution to jurisprudence: law requires determinate content, not suggestive ambiguity. The funeral example cleverly shows how the same word ('moderate') means different things to different people based on their circumstances -- the legislator must resolve this ambiguity into specific prescriptions. This anticipates modern debates about the determinacy of legal language and the need for precise statutory drafting.

narrative: "And is our legislator to have no preface to his laws, but to say at once Do this, avoid that -- and then holding the penalty in terrorem to go on to another law; offering never a word of advice or exhortation to those for whom he is legislating, after the manner of some doctors? For of doctors, as I may remind you, some have a gentler, others a ruder method of cure; and as children ask the doctor to be gentle with them, so we will ask the legislator to cure our disorders with the gentlest remedies. What I mean to say is, that besides doctors there are doctors' servants, who are also styled doctors."
refashioned:
"Should our legislator offer no preface to his laws, but simply say 'Do this, avoid that,' then hold the threat of punishment over the listener and move on to the next law -- never offering a word of advice or encouragement to those he is legislating for? That would be the manner of certain doctors. For as I may remind you, some doctors have a gentler method of treatment, and others a harsher one. Just as children ask their doctor to be gentle, so we will ask the legislator to cure our social disorders with the gentlest remedies possible.

What I mean to say is this: besides doctors in the full sense, there are doctors' assistants, who are also called doctors."
elegant: Should the legislator simply issue commands and threats without any preface of advice or encouragement -- like the harshest kind of doctor? Just as children ask the doctor to be gentle, we should ask the legislator to cure civic disorders with the gentlest remedies. There are two kinds of doctors, and the distinction illuminates legislation.
summary: The Athenian asks whether the legislator should issue bare commands backed by penalties, or first offer advice and encouragement. He introduces the analogy of two kinds of doctors -- one gentle, one harsh -- to argue that laws should be preceded by persuasive prefaces.
distilled: The legislator should not merely command and threaten but should preface laws with persuasive counsel, just as a good doctor uses gentle methods before harsh ones.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The medical analogy is one of the most important in the Laws and provides the theoretical foundation for the doctrine of legislative preambles. The distinction between doctors and doctors' servants (or assistants) maps onto the distinction between legislation with preambles (the freeman's doctor) and legislation without (the slave's doctor), developed fully in 4.38-4.39. In terrorem -- using threats to deter -- describes the punitive-only approach to law that the Athenian rejects. The medical metaphor for political care appears throughout Plato: Republic I (341c-342e) compares the ruler's art to the doctor's, and the Gorgias (464b-466a) distinguishes genuine medicine from flattery.

narrative:
"Very true."

"And whether they are slaves or freemen makes no difference; they acquire their knowledge of medicine by obeying and observing their masters; empirically and not according to the natural way of learning, as the manner of freemen is, who have learned scientifically themselves the art which they impart scientifically to their pupils. You are aware that there are these two classes of doctors?"

"To be sure."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"Whether these assistants are slaves or free men makes no difference: they acquire their medical knowledge by obeying and observing their masters -- empirically, not through the natural method of learning that free men follow, who learn the art scientifically themselves and impart it scientifically to their students. You are aware that there are these two classes of doctors?"

"Of course."
elegant: Medical assistants, whether slave or free, learn empirically by observing their masters rather than through systematic scientific understanding. This creates two fundamentally different classes of doctors: those who understand the principles of their art and those who merely follow experience.
summary: The Athenian distinguishes two classes of doctors: those who learn medicine scientifically and teach it systematically, and assistants who learn only by empirical observation of their masters. The distinction is not about social status but about depth of understanding.
distilled: The two classes of doctors are distinguished not by social status but by method of learning: scientific understanding versus mere empirical imitation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The distinction between empirical and scientific knowledge is fundamental to Greek epistemology. Aristotle develops it extensively in Metaphysics I.1 (981a-b), distinguishing experience (empeiria) from art (techne) and science (episteme). The claim that the assistants' knowledge is merely empirical -- based on habit and observation rather than understanding of causes -- parallels the Gorgias's distinction between genuine arts and empirical knacks (empeiriai, 462b-465d). The phrase "natural way of learning" (kata physin) implies that scientific understanding, not rote imitation, is how free minds properly acquire knowledge.

narrative: "And did you ever observe that there are two classes of patients in states, slaves and freemen; and the slave doctors run about and cure the slaves, or wait for them in the dispensaries -- practitioners of this sort never talk to their patients individually, or let them talk about their own individual complaints? The slave doctor prescribes what mere experience suggests, as if he had exact knowledge; and when he has given his orders, like a tyrant, he rushes off with equal assurance to some other servant who is ill; and so he relieves the master of the house of the care of his invalid slaves. But the other doctor, who is a freeman, attends and practises upon freemen; and he carries his enquiries far back, and goes into the nature of the disorder; he enters into discourse with the patient and with his friends, and is at once getting information from the sick man, and also instructing him as far as he is able, and he will not prescribe for him until he has first convinced him; at last, when he has brought the patient more and more under his persuasive influences and set him on the road to health, he attempts to effect a cure. Now which is the better way of proceeding in a physician and in a trainer? Is he the better who accomplishes his ends in a double way, or he who works in one way, and that the ruder and inferior?"
refashioned:
"Have you ever noticed that there are also two classes of patients in states -- slaves and free people? The slave doctors rush about treating slaves, or wait for them at dispensaries. These practitioners never speak with their patients individually or let patients describe their own complaints. The slave doctor prescribes whatever his experience suggests, as if he possessed exact knowledge. Once he has given his orders, like a tyrant, he dashes off with equal confidence to the next sick servant, relieving the master of the household from worrying about his ailing slaves.

But the other doctor -- a free man -- attends to free patients. He traces his inquiries far back and investigates the nature of the disorder. He enters into conversation with the patient and the patient's family, simultaneously gathering information from the sick person and instructing him as much as he can. He will not prescribe until he has first convinced the patient. Only after he has progressively won the patient over through persuasion and set him on the road to recovery does he attempt the actual cure.

Now, which is the better method for a doctor or a trainer? The one who achieves his aims through both persuasion and prescription, or the one who uses only the harsher, inferior method?"
elegant: The slave doctor rushes from patient to patient, prescribing by rote experience like a tyrant, never speaking with patients or hearing their complaints. The free doctor traces the disorder to its root, converses with the patient and family, gathers information and gives instruction, and prescribes nothing until he has first convinced the patient. Only then does he attempt the cure. The double method -- persuasion joined with prescription -- is clearly superior to command alone.
summary: The Athenian contrasts two medical practices: the slave doctor who prescribes tyrannically by experience alone, and the free doctor who investigates, converses, instructs, and convinces before prescribing. The free doctor's 'double method' of persuasion plus treatment is presented as clearly superior.
distilled: The free doctor who converses, investigates, and persuades before prescribing is superior to the slave doctor who commands by rote -- establishing persuasion-plus-authority as the model for legislation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage is the centerpiece of the Laws' argument for legislative preambles. The slave doctor functions as a metaphor for law-as-command (the coercive approach), while the free doctor represents law-with-preamble (the persuasive approach). The comparison of the slave doctor to a tyrant explicitly links the coercive style of legislation to tyrannical governance. The free doctor's method -- inquiry, conversation, instruction, conviction, then prescription -- maps onto the ideal legislative process: understand the situation, explain the reasoning, persuade the citizen, then command. This passage has been influential in legal philosophy, particularly in discussions of the expressive function of law and the role of reasons in legitimating legal authority. Compare Gorgias (464b-466a) on the distinction between genuine arts and flattery.

narrative:
"I should say, Stranger, that the double way is far better."

"Should you like to see an example of the double and single method in legislation?"

"Certainly I should."

"What will be our first law? Will not the order of nature, begin by making regulations for states about births?"

"He will."

"In all states the birth of children goes back to the connection of marriage?"

"Very true."

"And, according to the true order, the laws relating to marriage should be those which are first determined in every state?"

"Quite so."
refashioned:
"I would say, Stranger, that the double method is far better."

"Would you like to see an example of both the double and single method applied to legislation?"

"I certainly would."

"What will be our first law? Will the legislator not follow the order of nature and begin with regulations about births?"

"He will."

"In every state, the birth of children traces back to the institution of marriage?"

"Very true."

"And according to the natural order, the laws governing marriage should be the first ones established in any state?"

"Exactly so."
elegant: Cleinias affirms that the double method -- persuasion combined with command -- is far superior. They agree to demonstrate it with the first law any state should establish: following the order of nature, legislation must begin with marriage, since all births trace back to it.
summary: Cleinias agrees that the double method of persuasion plus command is superior. The Athenian proposes demonstrating both methods with the first law any state should enact: marriage law, since the natural order begins with births, which originate in marriage.
distilled: Having established the superiority of persuasion combined with command, the Athenian proposes to demonstrate both methods using marriage law -- the first law the natural order requires.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. -- The choice to begin legislation with marriage follows what the Athenian calls "the order of nature" (kata physin): since the city's fundamental need is for citizens, and citizens come from births, and births from marriages, marriage law logically comes first. This naturalistic approach to legislative ordering contrasts with the Republic's more radical treatment of marriage and reproduction among the guardian class (Republic V, 457c-461e), where marriages are arranged by the state for eugenic purposes. The Laws takes a more conventional approach, working within existing social institutions while reforming them. Beginning with marriage also echoes actual Greek legislative traditions, as many cities' law codes began with family law.

narrative: "Then let me first give the law of marriage in a simple form; it may run as follows: -- A man shall marry between the ages of thirty and thirty-five, or, if he does not, he shall pay such and such a fine, or shall suffer the loss of such and such privileges. This would be the simple law about marriage. The double law would run thus: -- A man shall marry between the ages of thirty and thirty-five, considering that in a manner the human race naturally partakes of immortality, which every man is by nature inclined to desire to the utmost; for the desire of every man that he may become famous, and not lie in the grave without a name, is only the love of continuance. Now mankind are coeval with all time, and are ever following, and will ever follow, the course of time; and so they are immortal, because they leave children's children behind them, and partake of immortality in the unity of generation. And for a man voluntarily to deprive himself of this gift, as he deliberately does who will not have a wife or children, is impiety. He who obeys the law shall be free, and shall pay no fine; but he who is disobedient, and does not marry, when he has arrived at the age of thirty-five, shall pay a yearly fine of a certain amount, in order that he may not imagine his celibacy to bring ease and profit to him; and he shall not share in the honours which the young men in the state give to the aged. Comparing now the two forms of the law, you will be able to arrive at a judgment about any other laws -- whether they should be double in length even when shortest, because they have to persuade as well as threaten, or whether they shall only threaten and be of half the length."
refashioned:
"Let me first state the marriage law in its simple form. It might run: 'A man shall marry between the ages of thirty and thirty-five. If he does not, he shall pay a specified fine or forfeit specified privileges.' That is the simple law.

The double law would run like this: 'A man shall marry between the ages of thirty and thirty-five, understanding that the human race naturally partakes of a kind of immortality, which every person is inclined by nature to desire as fully as possible. For the wish to become renowned and not to lie nameless in the grave is really the love of continuance. Humanity has existed alongside all time, follows the course of time, and will always follow it. In this sense we are immortal: we leave children's children behind us and share in immortality through the unity of generation. For a man to voluntarily deprive himself of this gift -- as he deliberately does when he refuses to marry or have children -- is an act of impiety. He who obeys the law shall be free and pay no fine. But he who disobeys and remains unmarried past thirty-five shall pay a yearly fine of a specified amount, so that he will not imagine his celibacy brings him ease and profit. He shall also be excluded from the honors that young men in the state customarily pay to their elders.'

Comparing these two forms, you can now judge about any law: whether it should be doubled in length, even at its shortest, because it must persuade as well as threaten -- or whether it should only threaten and be half as long."
elegant: The simple marriage law: marry between thirty and thirty-five, or pay a fine. The double law adds a preamble: humanity partakes of immortality through generation, leaving children's children who carry us forward through all time. To refuse marriage and children is to voluntarily abandon this immortality -- an act of impiety. The obedient pay no fine; the disobedient pay yearly and lose the honor of their juniors. This comparison reveals the choice every law presents: persuade and command, or merely command.
summary: The Athenian demonstrates the difference between simple and double legislation using marriage law. The simple version commands and threatens penalties. The double version first explains that marriage is how humanity participates in immortality, making voluntary celibacy an act of impiety, before stating the command and penalties.
distilled: The simple law commands marriage under penalty; the double law first persuades by explaining that children are humanity's share of immortality, making celibacy impious, then commands and penalizes.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This demonstration is the practical culmination of the entire prelude theory. The preamble to the marriage law appeals to the universal desire for continuance and fame, reframing marriage not as mere social obligation but as participation in human immortality through generation -- an argument that appears also in the Symposium (207a-208b), where Diotima explains that mortal creatures achieve immortality through reproduction. The characterization of voluntary celibacy as impiety is distinctive to the Laws and reflects its more conservative, communitarian ethos compared to the Republic. The marriage age of 30-35 for men is consistent with actual Athenian and Spartan practice. Compare Laws VI (772d-774a) where marriage legislation is developed in full detail.

narrative:
"The shorter form, Stranger, would be more in accordance with Lacedaemonian custom; although, for my own part, if any one were to ask me which I myself prefer in the state, I should certainly determine in favour of the longer; and I would have every law made after the same pattern, if I had to choose. But I think that Cleinias is the person to be consulted, for his is the state which is going to use these laws."

"Thank you, Megillus."
refashioned:
"The shorter form, Stranger, would be more consistent with Spartan custom. But for my own part, if anyone asked which I prefer in a state, I would certainly choose the longer form, and I would have every law follow the same pattern if the choice were mine. However, I think Cleinias is the one to consult, since his is the state that will actually use these laws."

"Thank you, Megillus."
elegant: Megillus notes that the shorter form suits Spartan custom, but personally prefers the longer, persuasive form for every law. He defers to Cleinias, whose state will use these laws.
summary: Megillus acknowledges that Sparta favors brevity in law, but personally prefers the double form with persuasive preambles. He defers the final judgment to Cleinias, since the laws are being designed for the new Cretan colony.
distilled: Megillus the Spartan personally prefers persuasive legislation over Sparta's characteristically terse laws, deferring the choice to Cleinias whose colony will use them.
notes: Speakers: Megillus and Cleinias. -- Megillus's acknowledgment that Spartan custom favors brevity is historically accurate: the Spartans were famous for laconic speech (the word 'laconic' derives from Laconia, the region around Sparta). Plutarch's Life of Lycurgus (13) reports that Lycurgus deliberately made Spartan laws brief and unwritten, relying on education rather than legal texts. Megillus's personal preference for the longer form is significant: even a Spartan, trained to value brevity, recognizes the superiority of persuasive legislation. His deference to Cleinias is both courteous and politically appropriate, since the laws are being designed for Cleinias's Cretan colony, not for Sparta.

narrative:
"Whether, in the abstract, words are to be many or few, is a very foolish question; the best form, and not the shortest, is to be approved; nor is length at all to be regarded. Of the two forms of law which have been recited, the one is not only twice as good in practical usefulness as the other, but the case is like that of the two kinds of doctors, which I was just now mentioning. And yet legislators never appear to have considered that they have two instruments which they might use in legislation -- persuasion and force; for in dealing with the rude and uneducated multitude, they use the one only as far as they can; they do not mingle persuasion with coercion, but employ force pure and simple. Moreover, there is a third point, sweet friends, which ought to be, and never is, regarded in our existing laws."

"What is it?"
refashioned:
"Whether laws should use many words or few is a completely foolish question. The best form, not the shortest, is what should be approved. Length is irrelevant. Of the two forms of law we have just heard, the longer one is not merely twice as useful in practice as the shorter -- the case is exactly like that of the two kinds of doctors I described.

And yet legislators never seem to have recognized that they possess two instruments for legislation: persuasion and force. When dealing with the uneducated masses, they use only one. They do not blend persuasion with coercion but rely on force alone.

There is also a third point, dear friends, which ought to be considered in our laws but never is."

"What is it?"
elegant: Whether laws use many or few words is a foolish question -- only the best form matters, not the shortest. The persuasive law is not merely twice as useful as the bare command; it is as superior as the free doctor to the slave doctor. Yet legislators have never recognized that they possess two instruments -- persuasion and force -- and use only force. A third, overlooked point remains.
summary: The Athenian dismisses the question of legislative brevity as foolish -- quality matters, not length. He observes that legislators have never realized they have two instruments (persuasion and force) and have relied exclusively on force. He hints at a third overlooked principle.
distilled: Length is irrelevant to good legislation; what matters is combining persuasion with force, two instruments legislators have never thought to blend, plus a third principle yet to be revealed.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The dismissal of brevity as a standard anticipates modern debates about the relative merits of concise versus detailed legislation. The observation that legislators have "two instruments" (persuasion and force) but use only one is the Laws' central critique of existing legal practice. The word "sweet friends" (o philoi) marks an affectionate moment in the dialogue. The mysterious "third point" about to be introduced will turn out to be the concept of the preamble itself -- the discovery that what they have been doing all along (the preceding discussion) is itself a form of legislative prelude. This is a characteristic Platonic moment of retrospective recognition.

narrative:
"A point arising out of our previous discussion, which comes into my mind in some mysterious way. All this time, from early dawn until noon, have we been talking about laws in this charming retreat: now we are going to promulgate our laws, and what has preceded was only the prelude of them. Why do I mention this? For this reason: -- Because all discourses and vocal exercises have preludes and overtures, which are a sort of artistic beginnings intended to help the strain which is to be performed; lyric measures and music of every other kind have preludes framed with wonderful care. But of the truer and higher strain of law and politics, no one has ever yet uttered any prelude, or composed or published any, as though there was no such thing in nature. Whereas our present discussion seems to me to imply that there is; -- these double laws, of which we were speaking, are not exactly double, but they are in two parts, the law and the prelude of the law. The arbitrary command, which was compared to the commands of doctors, whom we described as of the meaner sort, was the law pure and simple; and that which preceded, and was described by our friend here as being hortatory only, was, although in fact, an exhortation, likewise analogous to the preamble of a discourse. For I imagine that all this language of conciliation, which the legislator has been uttering in the preface of the law, was intended to create goodwill in the person whom he addressed, in order that, by reason of this good-will, he might more intelligently receive his command, that is to say, the law. And therefore, in my way of speaking, this is more rightly described as the preamble than as the matter of the law. And I must further proceed to observe, that to all his laws, and to each separately, the legislator should prefix a preamble; he should remember how great will be the difference between them, according as they have, or have not, such preambles, as in the case already given."

"The lawgiver, if he asks my opinion, will certainly legislate in the form which you advise."
refashioned:
"A point arising from our previous discussion comes to mind in an almost mysterious way. All this time, from early morning until noon, we have been discussing laws in this pleasant setting. Now we are about to actually promulgate our laws -- and everything that preceded was merely the prelude to them.

Why do I point this out? Because all discourses and musical performances have preludes and overtures -- artistic beginnings designed to prepare the listener for the main work. Lyric compositions and every other kind of music have preludes crafted with extraordinary care. Yet in the higher and truer art of law and politics, no one has ever composed or published a prelude, as though no such thing existed in nature.

But our present discussion proves that it does exist. These 'double laws' we have been describing are not really double -- they have two parts: the law itself and the prelude to the law. The bare command, which we compared to the prescriptions of the inferior kind of doctor, is the law pure and simple. The part that preceded it, which our friend here described as merely hortatory, is in fact the preamble -- analogous to the introduction of a speech.

All the conciliatory language the legislator uses in this preface is designed to create goodwill in the person addressed, so that the goodwill makes him receive the command -- the law itself -- more intelligently. Therefore this persuasive element is more properly called the preamble than part of the law's substance.

I must add that the legislator should prefix a preamble to all his laws, each one individually. The difference it makes -- whether laws have such preambles or not -- is enormous, as our example has already shown."

"The lawgiver, if he asks my opinion, will certainly legislate in the form you recommend."
elegant: Everything discussed from dawn until noon was prelude, not legislation proper. Music and speeches all have preludes, yet no one has ever composed preludes for law and politics -- as if they did not exist in nature. But our discussion proves they do. The 'double law' has two parts: the preamble that creates goodwill and the command that follows. The preamble prepares the listener to receive the law intelligently. Every law should have one, for the difference between laws with and without preambles is enormous.
summary: The Athenian makes the retrospective discovery that their entire morning's discussion was itself a legislative prelude. Just as music has overtures, so law should have preambles that create goodwill and prepare citizens to receive commands intelligently. The 'double law' is really law plus preamble, and every individual law should be prefaced this way.
distilled: The morning's entire discussion was itself a legislative preamble -- and just as music has overtures, every law should have a prelude that creates goodwill and prepares citizens to receive the command intelligently.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This passage contains the Laws' most important methodological discovery: the concept of the legislative preamble (prooimion). The retrospective realization that the discussion was itself a prelude is a characteristic Platonic dramatic device -- the characters discover the theoretical framework for what they have already been doing. The musical analogy (prooimion literally means 'before the song') grounds legal preambles in an existing artistic practice, suggesting that legislation is itself an art form. The distinction between the preamble (which persuades) and the law proper (which commands) has been enormously influential in legal philosophy and constitutional theory. The claim that no one before has composed preludes for law amounts to Plato asserting originality for this contribution -- a rare moment of explicit self-consciousness in the dialogues. Compare Republic III (398c-403c) on the relationship between music and legislation.

narrative: "I think that you are right, Cleinias, in affirming that all laws have preambles, and that throughout the whole of this work of legislation every single law should have a suitable preamble at the beginning; for that which is to follow is most important, and it makes all the difference whether we clearly remember the preambles or not. Yet we should be wrong in requiring that all laws, small and great alike, should have preambles of the same kind, any more than all songs or speeches; although they may be natural to all, they are not always necessary, and whether they are to be employed or not has in each case to be left to the judgment of the speaker or the musician, or, in the present instance, of the lawgiver."
refashioned:
"I think you are right, Cleinias, to affirm that all laws should have preambles and that throughout this entire work of legislation every individual law should have a suitable preamble at the beginning. What follows is of the greatest importance, and it makes all the difference whether we clearly remember the preambles or not.

However, we would be wrong to insist that every law, small and great alike, must have a preamble of the same kind -- just as we would be wrong to require the same of all songs or speeches. Preambles may be natural to all laws, but they are not always necessary. Whether to use them, and in what form, must in each case be left to the judgment of the speaker, the musician, or -- in our case -- the lawgiver."
elegant: Every law should ideally have a suitable preamble, and it makes all the difference whether citizens remember them. But not every law, great or small, requires the same kind of preamble -- just as not every song or speech does. The decision to include a preamble and what form it takes must be left to the lawgiver's judgment.
summary: The Athenian endorses the principle that all laws should have preambles but adds an important qualification: not every law requires a preamble of the same kind or length. Like the musician or speaker, the lawgiver must exercise judgment about when and how to employ them.
distilled: All laws should ideally have preambles, but not all require the same kind -- the lawgiver must exercise judgment about when and how to use them, just as a musician judges when a prelude is needed.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This qualification prevents the preamble doctrine from becoming rigid and mechanical. The comparison to musical and rhetorical judgment introduces the concept of legislative discretion (kairos -- the right moment and manner) as essential to good lawgiving. Small or self-evident laws may not need elaborate persuasion; major or counterintuitive laws may need extensive preambles. This flexible approach distinguishes the Laws from the more systematic and comprehensive Republic. The emphasis on remembering preambles connects to the broader Platonic theme that understanding (not mere compliance) is the goal of education and legislation alike.

narrative: "That I think is most true. And now, Stranger, without delay let us return to the argument, and, as people say in play, make a second and better beginning, if you please, with the principles which we have been laying down, which we never thought of regarding as a preamble before, but of which we may now make a preamble, and not merely consider them to be chance topics of discourse. Let us acknowledge, then, that we have a preamble. About the honour of the Gods and the respect of parents, enough has been already said; and we may proceed to the topics which follow next in order, until the preamble is deemed by you to be complete; and after that you shall go through the laws themselves."
refashioned:
"I think that is entirely true. Now, Stranger, let us return to the argument without delay. As people say in games, let us make a second and better beginning -- if you will -- with the principles we have been developing. We never thought of them as a preamble before, but we can now recognize them as one rather than treating them as random topics of conversation.

Let us acknowledge, then, that we have our preamble. On the honor of the gods and respect for parents, enough has already been said. We may proceed to the topics that follow next in order, until you consider the preamble complete. After that, you shall take us through the laws themselves."
elegant: Cleinias proposes making a fresh start by recognizing their previous discussion as the preamble it always was, rather than treating it as chance conversation. The sections on honoring the gods and respecting parents are complete; the remaining topics should be addressed until the preamble is finished, after which actual legislation begins.
summary: Cleinias suggests they make a fresh start by formally recognizing their preceding discussion as the legislative preamble it has turned out to be. The sections on divine honor and parental respect are complete, and they should continue the preamble's remaining topics before proceeding to the laws themselves.
distilled: Cleinias proposes reframing their entire preceding discussion as the formal preamble to legislation, with divine honor and parental respect already covered and the remaining topics to follow before actual lawgiving begins.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- The phrase "second and better beginning" (deuteros plous -- literally 'second sailing') was a Greek proverb meaning a fresh attempt that benefits from what was learned in the first. Cleinias's suggestion demonstrates that the concept of the legislative preamble has been successfully communicated: he now sees the structure that the Athenian revealed. The acknowledgment that their discussion was not "chance topics of discourse" but a coherent preamble retroactively transforms the entire preceding conversation. This moment of recognition parallels the philosopher's ascent in the Allegory of the Cave (Republic VII, 514a-517a) -- seeing clearly what was there all along.

narrative:
"I understand you to mean that we have made a sufficient preamble about Gods and demi-gods, and about parents living or dead; and now you would have us bring the rest of the subject into the light of day?"

"Exactly."

"After this, as is meet and for the interest of us all, I the speaker, and you the listeners, will try to estimate all that relates to the souls and bodies and properties of the citizens, as regards both their occupations and arrive, as far as in us lies, at the nature of education. These then are the topics which follow next in order."

"Very good."
refashioned:
"I understand you to mean that we have completed the preamble's treatment of gods, demigods, and parents both living and dead, and that you now want us to bring the remaining subjects to light?"

"Exactly."

"Then after this, as is fitting and in the interest of us all, I as speaker and you as listeners will attempt to assess everything that concerns the souls, bodies, and property of the citizens, including their occupations, and arrive as best we can at the nature of education. These are the topics that come next in order."

"Very good."
elegant: The preamble's treatment of gods, demigods, and parents is complete. What follows concerns the souls, bodies, and property of the citizens -- their occupations and, above all, the nature of education.
summary: The Athenian confirms the preamble on divine and parental obligations is complete and announces the next topics: the souls, bodies, and property of citizens, their occupations, and the nature of education -- transitioning toward Book 5.
distilled: With the preamble on gods and parents complete, the discussion turns to the citizens' souls, bodies, property, occupations, and education -- the subjects that will occupy the remaining books.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This closing passage establishes the agenda for Book 5 and beyond: souls, bodies, and property represent the three domains of human life that legislation must address, listed in order of importance (soul first, property last). The promise to investigate "the nature of education" points toward the extended educational discussions in Books 7 and 12. The tripartite structure (soul, body, property) echoes the Republic's tripartite psychology (reason, spirit, appetite) mapped onto social structure. The phrase "bring into the light of day" recalls the Allegory of the Cave's imagery of emerging from darkness into illumination (Republic VII, 515c-516b). Book 4 thus ends with a clear transition: the theoretical foundations are laid, and the practical work of legislation is about to begin.
